questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a typical etiology of chronic pancreatitis?
questions.explanation: Gallstones are the leading cause of acute pancreatitis but are rarely a primary cause of chronic pancreatitis, which is most commonly caused by alcohol abuse.

questions.text: A patient presents with acute abdominal pain. Serum amylase is elevated. Which of the following conditions is associated with an elevated amylase but typically normal serum lipase?
questions.explanation: Amylase is produced by both the pancreas and salivary glands. Parotitis causes isolated hyperamylasemia (salivary origin). Lipase is specific to the pancreas and remains normal in salivary gland pathology.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a recognized complication of acute pancreatitis?
questions.explanation: Hypocalcemia is a complication of pancreatitis (due to fat saponification). Hypercalcemia is a potential cause of pancreatitis, not a result.

questions.text: All of the following are recognized causes of acute pancreatitis EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Hypercalcemia, not hypocalcemia, is a metabolic cause of acute pancreatitis. Hypocalcemia is a potential complication of severe acute pancreatitis due to saponification of fats in the peritoneum.

questions.text: Pancreatitis Which of the following is the most sensitive and specific serum biomarker for the diagnosis of acute pancreatitis?
questions.explanation: Serum lipase is preferred over serum amylase because it remains elevated longer and has higher sensitivity and specificity for pancreatic tissue injury. Amylase can rise in non-pancreatic conditions and drops quickly.

questions.text: Which of the following scoring systems is specifically designed to assess the severity of acute pancreatitis at the time of admission and monitor progress over the first 48 hours using criteria such as WBC count, blood glucose, and fluid sequestration?
questions.explanation: Ranson criteria evaluate 5 parameters at admission and 6 parameters during the initial 48 hours to predict the severity and mortality risk of acute pancreatitis. The other scales are for stroke, liver failure, pneumonia, or consciousness.

questions.text: A 45-year-old patient with severe acute pancreatitis develops a persistent high fever and worsening abdominal pain 2 weeks after admission. A CT scan reveals gas bubbles within a well-defined pancreatic collection. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Conservative management with intravenous fluids only
Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and planning for drainage
originalOptions: Conservative management with intravenous fluids only
Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and planning for drainage
questions.correctAnswer: C) Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and planning for drainage
questions.correctAnswerText: Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and planning for drainage
questions.explanation: The presence of gas bubbles within a fluid collection indicates infected pancreatic necrosis or abscess. This requires targeted antibiotic treatment (such as carbapenems) along with minimally invasive or surgical necrosectomy/drainage.

questions.text: Which of the following physical examination findings is characterized by ecchymosis around the umbilicus, indicating severe necrotizing pancreatitis with retroperitoneal hemorrhage?
questions.explanation: Cullen's sign is superficial bruising around the umbilicus. Grey Turner's sign refers to bruising along the flanks. Both signs represent retroperitoneal bleeding associated with severe hemorrhagic pancreatitis.

questions.text: What is the classic clinical triad used to recognize advanced chronic pancreatitis?
options: Jaundice, weight loss, and palpable gallbladder
Steatorrhea, pancreatic calcifications, and diabetes mellitus
Abdominal pain, ascites, and pleural effusion
Fever, right upper quadrant pain, and jaundice
originalOptions: Jaundice, weight loss, and palpable gallbladder
Steatorrhea, pancreatic calcifications, and diabetes mellitus
Abdominal pain, ascites, and pleural effusion
Fever, right upper quadrant pain, and jaundice
questions.correctAnswer: B) Steatorrhea, pancreatic calcifications, and diabetes mellitus
questions.correctAnswerText: Steatorrhea, pancreatic calcifications, and diabetes mellitus
questions.explanation: This classic triad signifies the loss of both exocrine function (causing fat malabsorption/steatorrhea) and endocrine function (causing diabetes) due to extensive parenchymal destruction and calcification over time.

questions.text: A patient with a history of recurrent acute pancreatitis is found to have significantly elevated serum IgG4 levels. Which type of pancreatitis is most strongly associated with this finding?
questions.explanation: Type 1 Autoimmune Pancreatitis is part of an IgG4-related systemic disease. It responds well to steroid therapy and characteristically shows elevated serum levels of IgG4 antibodies.

questions.text: Which of the following pathogenic mechanisms initiates the tissue damage seen in acute pancreatitis?
options: Bacterial infection of the biliary tree
Premature intra-acinar activation of trypsinogen to trypsin
Ischemic injury due to mesenteric artery occlusion
Excessive secretion of bicarbonate by pancreatic ductal cells
originalOptions: Bacterial infection of the biliary tree
Premature intra-acinar activation of trypsinogen to trypsin
Ischemic injury due to mesenteric artery occlusion
Excessive secretion of bicarbonate by pancreatic ductal cells
questions.correctAnswer: B) Premature intra-acinar activation of trypsinogen to trypsin
questions.correctAnswerText: Premature intra-acinar activation of trypsinogen to trypsin
questions.explanation: The cornerstone of acute pancreatitis pathogenesis is the inappropriate, premature activation of digestive zymogens (specifically trypsinogen to trypsin) within the pancreatic acinar cells, leading to autodigestion of the gland.

questions.text: All of the following are recognized causes of acute pancreatitis EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Hypercalcemia, not hypocalcemia, is a metabolic cause of acute pancreatitis. Hypocalcemia is a potential complication of severe acute pancreatitis due to saponification of fats in the peritoneum.

questions.text: Back Pain Which of the following is an accurate description of mechanical back pain?
options: Low back pain that is worse with prolonged standing and relieved by ambulation
Low back pain that is worse at rest and relieved by activity
Low back pain associated with fever and night sweats
Low back pain with saddle anaesthesia
originalOptions: Low back pain that is worse with prolonged standing and relieved by ambulation
Low back pain that is worse at rest and relieved by activity
Low back pain associated with fever and night sweats
Low back pain with saddle anaesthesia
questions.correctAnswer: A) Low back pain that is worse with prolonged standing and relieved by ambulation
questions.correctAnswerText: Low back pain that is worse with prolonged standing and relieved by ambulation

questions.text: A 45-year-old worker presents with a 4-week history of lower back pain. The pain is isolated to his lower back and he is not experiencing any signs of radiculopathy. What is the best next step in management?
On MRI of the lumbar spine, a disc herniation at L4/L5 is identified compressing a nerve root. What is the most likely level affected?
What are the most common symptoms of cervical myelopathy?
options: Bilateral hand weakness, numbness and pain (radiculopathy)
Bilateral hand clumsiness and gait instability
originalOptions: Bilateral hand weakness, numbness and pain (radiculopathy)
Bilateral hand clumsiness and gait instability
questions.correctAnswer: B) Bilateral hand clumsiness and gait instability
questions.correctAnswerText: Bilateral hand clumsiness and gait instability

questions.text: A patient presents with loss of sensation below the umbilicus, paraplegia, and stool/urine incontinence. What is the most likely level of injury?
options: Severe head trauma affecting motor area
originalOptions: Severe head trauma affecting motor area

questions.text: Which of the following tests does NOT evaluate spinal nerve root compression?
Which nerve root is affected by a C5/C6 disc herniation?
CNS Tumours What is the most common primary brain tumor in adults?
Which of the following tumors has the WORST prognosis (mean survival)?
Which of the following statements regarding Medulloblastoma is WRONG?
options: It is the most common malignant primary brain tumor in children
It is a small cell embryonal tumor that typically arises in the cerebellar vermis
It is classified as WHO Grade I because it occurs in children
It often presents with signs and symptoms of hydrocephalus
originalOptions: It is the most common malignant primary brain tumor in children
It is a small cell embryonal tumor that typically arises in the cerebellar vermis
It is classified as WHO Grade I because it occurs in children
It often presents with signs and symptoms of hydrocephalus
questions.correctAnswer: C) It is classified as WHO Grade I because it occurs in children
questions.correctAnswerText: It is classified as WHO Grade I because it occurs in children

questions.text: Headache What is the most likely diagnosis?
What is the treatment for temporal arteritis?
Which of the following is the drug of choice for prophylaxis of episodic and chronic cluster headache?
Subarachnoid Haemorrhage What is the most sensitive test for detection of acute subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)?
A 62-year-old female patient was admitted to the ICU with aneurysmal SAH. 6 days later, she developed sudden aphasia and right-sided hemiplegia. What is the most likely cause?
options: Rebleeding from anterior communicating artery aneurysm
originalOptions: Rebleeding from anterior communicating artery aneurysm

questions.text: Which of the following is WRONG regarding intracranial aneurysms?
options: More common in males than in females
Rupture occurs in 95-97% of symptomatic cases
Berry aneurysms account for 75-80% of SAH
originalOptions: More common in males than in females
Rupture occurs in 95-97% of symptomatic cases
Berry aneurysms account for 75-80% of SAH
questions.correctAnswer: B) More common in males than in females
questions.correctAnswerText: More common in males than in females

questions.text: A patient with SAH has GCS of 13-14 with no major deficit. What is the WFNS grade?
All of the following are in favor of surgical clipping over coiling in the management of intracranial aneurysms EXCEPT:
options: Young patient with long life expectancy
Hunt and Hess grade 4 on admission
originalOptions: Young patient with long life expectancy
Hunt and Hess grade 4 on admission
questions.correctAnswer: C) Hunt and Hess grade 4 on admission
questions.correctAnswerText: Hunt and Hess grade 4 on admission

questions.text: What is the treatment for vasospasm after SAH?
questions.correctAnswer: A) Triple H therapy (Hemodilution, Hypervolemia, Hypertension)
